title: Growth Experimentation Maturity

introduction.subtitle: Experimentation maturity assessment. 6 min.
introduction.description:
Growth Experimentation Maturity helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Measurement & Metric Quality, Portfolio & Prioritisation, Experiment Design, Learning Velocity. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

The score stays separate from the optional answer-support check, which asks whether your answers are based on opinion, anecdotes, documentation, or system-verified evidence. A high answer should mean the practice is visible, repeatable, and useful in real decisions.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.duration: 8 diagnostic questions plus 4 evidence checks. Approximately 6 min.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How reliable is measurement across the critical growth funnel?

options.text: A. The answer relies mainly on opinion, pitch, memory, or isolated examples.
B. There are supporting examples, but sources are partial, informal, or hard to compare.
C. There is documented evidence from relevant customers, operations, metrics, or records.
D. The evidence is triangulated, current, owner-reviewed, and strong enough to change a material decision.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Measurement & Metric Quality: How reliable is measurement across the critical growth funnel
conceptHelp.summary: Measurement & Metric Quality means whether growth metrics are instrumented, trusted, tied to the funnel, and protected by guardrails. This question narrows the concept to "How reliable is measurement across the critical growth funnel?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Measurement & Metric Quality matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show how the team answers "How reliable is measurement across the critical growth funnel?" today, who owns it, what evidence supports it, and one recent decision or trade-off it changed.

questions.text: How are success and guardrail metrics chosen?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How is the experiment backlog created?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Portfolio & Prioritisation; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Portfolio & Prioritisation, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Portfolio & Prioritisation has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Portfolio & Prioritisation is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Portfolio & Prioritisation: How is the experiment backlog created
conceptHelp.summary: Portfolio & Prioritisation means choosing experiments as a balanced portfolio of expected impact, effort, risk, and learning value. This question narrows the concept to "How is the experiment backlog created?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Experiment Design; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.

conceptHelp.title: Experiment Design: How specific is the experiment hypothesis
conceptHelp.summary: Experiment Design means defining a test so the team can interpret cause, success, failure, and next action instead of just running activity. This question narrows the concept to "How specific is the experiment hypothesis?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

questions.text: How rapidly does the team complete decision-relevant learning cycles?

options.text: A. It happens rarely, reactively, or only when a crisis forces attention.
B. It happens occasionally, but timing depends on individual initiative or urgency.
C. There is a defined cadence with clear participants, owners, and triggers.
D. The cadence is frequent enough to change decisions, includes quality checks, and improves from outcomes.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Learning Velocity: How rapidly does the team complete decision-relevant learning cycles
conceptHelp.summary: Learning Velocity means how quickly the team turns experiments into decisions, records what was learned, and avoids repeating the same uncertainty. This question narrows the concept to "How rapidly does the team complete decision-relevant learning cycles?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

contextQuestions.text: What level of evidence supports your answers for Measurement & Metric Quality?

options.text: Opinion: mostly judgement, memory, or individual perception.
Anecdotal: examples exist, but they are not systematically documented.
Documented: there are current notes, models, policies, metrics, or records.
System-verified: evidence is routinely produced by a governed workflow, system, or review cadence.

conceptHelp.title: Measurement & Metric Quality
conceptHelp.summary: This question asks how well your answers about Measurement & Metric Quality are supported. Measurement & Metric Quality means whether growth metrics are instrumented, trusted, tied to the funnel, and protected by guardrails. The goal is learning: separate what you know from what you suspect, remember, or still need to verify.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A result about Measurement & Metric Quality is more useful when you know whether it rests on opinion, a few examples, current documents, or system-verified evidence. Weak support does not make the answer useless; it tells you what to learn next.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the strongest support you actually have right now. Do not upgrade an answer because the practice feels important, and do not downgrade it if current records or systems really exist.
conceptHelp.example: If your Measurement & Metric Quality answer is based on a recent metric, policy, deal document, customer record, or operating review, choose documented or system-verified. If it is mostly memory or judgement, choose opinion or anecdotal.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
contextDescription: This step is not scored. It records what your diagnostic answers are based on: opinion, anecdote, documentation, or system-verified evidence. Use it to decide how much to verify the result before acting on it.
